summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

In genetics, what is a carrier?

A carrier is heterozygous — they have one dominant and one recessive allele. The dominant allele masks the recessive one, so the carrier appears unaffected, but they can pass the recessive allele to offspring. This is especially important for X-linked traits, where carrier mothers can pass the allele to sons who will express it.

A woman who is a carrier for color blindness (X^C X^c) has children with a man who has normal vision (X^C Y). What is the probability that a DAUGHTER will be color blind?

Daughters receive one X from each parent. Father always gives X^C (normal). Mother gives either X^C or X^c. So daughters are either X^C X^C (normal) or X^C X^c (carrier, but pheno­typically normal). No daughter can be X^c X^c because the father cannot contribute X^c. To have a color-blind daughter, the father would also need to be color blind (X^c Y).

A color-blind man (X^c Y) marries a woman with normal vision whose father was color blind. What is the probability that their SON will be color blind?

The woman's father was color blind (X^c Y), so he must have given her X^c. Since she has normal vision, her genotype must be X^C X^c (carrier). Crossed with a color-blind man (X^c Y): sons get their X from the mother — either X^C (normal) or X^c (color blind). So 50% of sons will be color blind. The father's X^c goes only to daughters, not sons (sons get Y from dad).

summary_sections.title: Outliers and Resistant Measures
summary_sections.content_html:
Outliers and Resistant Measures

Possible offspring: X^H X^H (normal daughter), X^H X^h (carrier daughter), X^H Y (normal son), X^h Y (hemophiliac son). Only one out of four possible outcomes produces hemophilia (the affected son). So 25% of ALL children are expected to be hemophiliac. Note: 50% of SONS will be affected, but across all children it is 25%.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A person with type AB blood expresses both A antigens and B antigens on their red blood cells. This is an example of:

In codominance, both alleles are fully and simultaneously expressed in the heterozygote. A person with genotype IᴬIᴮ displays BOTH A and B antigens — they are not blended into an intermediate, but both are distinctly present.

Example: The ABO blood group system has three alleles in the population: Iᴬ, Iᴮ, and i. This is an example of:

Multiple alleles means more than two alleles exist in the population for a single gene, even though each individual only carries two. The ABO system has three alleles (Iᴬ, Iᴮ, and i), producing four possible blood types: A, B, AB, and O.

Example: Sickle cell disease is caused by one gene mutation but affects many organs including the blood, spleen, kidneys, and brain. This is an example of:

Pleiotropy is when a single gene influences multiple, seemingly unrelated traits. The sickle cell gene (HbS) alters hemoglobin shape, which causes a cascade of effects throughout the body — anemia, organ damage, pain crises, and more — all from one mutation.

Example: Which of the following best defines the term 'multiple alleles'?

Multiple alleles means more than two versions of a gene exist in the population. The ABO blood type system is the best example: three alleles (Iᴬ, Iᴮ, i) exist, but any one person only has two of them. This is different from polygenic inheritance, where many genes (not many alleles of one gene) affect a trait.

Example: A person who is heterozygous for the sickle cell allele (HbA HbS) has both normal and sickle-shaped red blood cells. They generally do not show severe symptoms and have increased resistance to malaria. This heterozygote advantage, combined with the fact that one gene (HbS) causes effects in blood, spleen, kidneys, and lungs, demonstrates which TWO non-Mendelian patterns?

The sickle cell trait demonstrates codominance at the molecular level — heterozygotes produce BOTH normal hemoglobin (HbA) and sickle hemoglobin (HbS), not an intermediate form. It also demonstrates pleiotropy because the single HbS gene mutation affects multiple organ systems (blood, spleen, kidneys, lungs, brain). The sickle cell gene is autosomal (chromosome 11), not X-linked.
